title: Opening: The Best Case Scenario
synopsis: Dee Hankins opens the Elevate Summit by asking every entrepreneur in the room to close their eyes and picture their best case scenario — the hammock, the car, the compound, the freedom. Then he introduces Jon Cobb and sets the tone for two days of honest, grounded work.
takeaways: Your best case scenario is absolutely possible — the only question is whether you'll commit to it
Entrepreneurship should be a team sport, not a lonely road
The gap between doers and complainers is growing every day — choose your side
Invest 5–10% of your revenue back into yourself: conferences, courses, coaching
Today is day one — not someday
who: Entrepreneurs who need a reset before they can receive what's coming
brief: Inspirational speaker and emcee Dee Hankins on why your best case scenario is closer than you think.

title: From Homeless to Harvard: The Master Cycle for Business Growth
synopsis: Jon Cobb tells the unvarnished story of dropping out at 16, joining the Marines, crashing the car he was living in, and hearing Les Brown say two words — 'it's possible' — that changed everything. He then introduces the Master Cycle: a seven-bucket framework covering personal development, leadership, sales, operations, marketing, feedback, and vision that any business can use to find and fix its weakest link.
takeaways: You don't need to believe you'll be a millionaire — you just need to crack the door: 'it's possible'
Edu-action = education + action. Learning without applying is just expensive procrastination
Every business faces the same seven challenges — the Master Cycle maps all of them
Self-leadership is the most neglected skill in entrepreneurship: lead yourself first
Sales is the magic most entrepreneurs are missing — if you can't sell it, you can't prove how good it is
Anonymous feedback is the only kind that tells the truth
Write your vision down and make it plain — a goal without specifics is a wish
who: Business owners who feel stuck, overwhelmed, or like they're carrying it all alone
brief: Business strategist and Elevate Summit founder Jon Cobb on the framework that has taken multiple businesses to $100M+.

title: The Market Cannot Reward What It Cannot See
synopsis: Danesha Mo'Nek — psychologist turned $5M content creator — explains why expertise without visibility is invisible money. Starting from a homeless shelter and reaching $10K/day in 90 days by posting consistently, she breaks down the 7-11-4 rule (7 hours of content, 11 touchpoints, 4 platforms) and the neuroscience of parasocial trust. The content doesn't have to be perfect. It has to be present.
takeaways: The 7-11-4 rule: buyers need 7 hours of content, 11 interactions, and 4 platforms before they trust you enough to buy
Parasocial relationships are real — your audience feels like they know you before they meet you
You don't need a big following. You need the right following. One thousand true fans beat a million passive scrollers
Post the same message a thousand different ways — repetition builds the brand, variety keeps it alive
Personal brand and product brand must be separate — one can outlive the other
TikTok's algorithm rewards completion rate, not follower count — hook in the first three seconds
Authenticity is not the same as oversharing — it's consistency of character across every platform
who: Entrepreneurs who know their expertise but struggle to be seen and trusted at scale
brief: Content strategist and self-esteem coach Danesha Mo'Nek on why visibility is the missing variable in most business equations.

title: Remove the Armor: Rewiring the Brain for Visibility and Growth
synopsis: Dr. Negin Rajaipour — mindset coach and neuroscientist — traces every entrepreneur's fear of visibility back to a childhood moment when the brain captured danger and built armor to protect against it. Using live audience exercises (the spider visualization, the inner voice reframe), she teaches how to identify and remove three pieces of armor: the Helmet (worst-case scanning), the Cloak (playing small), and the Shield (avoiding connection). She then plays a private video she recorded for herself — never meant to be seen — from the moment she quit her Silicon Valley job to become a speaker. Jon Cobb was in the room when she won a PR raffle at a sales event. They are now married and run Elevate Summit together.
takeaways: The brain doesn't distinguish between a remembered threat and a real one — your stress response fires the same way either way
The Helmet: scanning for worst-case scenarios to protect you from pain you experienced as a child
The Cloak: playing small, downplaying expertise, waiting until you feel ready — which never comes
The Shield: avoiding the conversation that could change your life because connection feels risky
Your body gets addicted to familiar emotions the same way it gets addicted to drugs — change feels like withdrawal
Visualization changes the brain physiologically: basketball players who only visualized free throws improved almost as much as those who practiced daily
Put distance between yourself and the problem — literally imagine it shrinking across the room — and your nervous system shifts from threat to clarity
The river of change is real: when your body fights back hardest is when you're halfway there. Don't turn around
who: Entrepreneurs who know what to do but can't make themselves do it — and wonder why
brief: Dr. Negin Rajaipour on the neuroscience of why smart entrepreneurs stay hidden, and exactly how to take the armor off.

title: Key Singularity: AI as a Business Multiplier
synopsis: Karl Kimball — former bank executive turned AI pioneer — maps where AI sits in 2026 and where it's going. Using Jevons Paradox (when something gets cheaper, people use more of it, not less), he argues AI won't eliminate jobs — it will 10x demand. He introduces four levels of AI adoption: thought partner, assistant, teammate, and operating language — and argues the biggest leverage for any small business is moving from level two to level three.
takeaways: Jevons Paradox: when AI makes work cheaper and faster, demand for that work goes up — not down
Four levels of AI: thought partner (ask it things) → assistant (do things for me) → teammate (own a job end-to-end) → operating language (coordinates the whole company)
Most businesses are at level one or two. The biggest ROI is the jump to level three
AI creates ROI on work that is expensive, slow, inconsistent, or capacity-constrained
Voice is the next user interface — typing will be the relic
The singularity isn't a moment in the future. We are in it right now
You don't have to build your own AI — find someone who already built what you need and plug it in
who: Business owners ready to move from experimenting with AI to actually deploying it
brief: AI Pioneer and Co-Founder of Key Singularity Karl Kimball on the four levels of AI adoption and where the real leverage is.

title: Key Singularity: AI Operations in Practice
synopsis: Kristi Galus — operations expert with deep manufacturing and industrial experience — shows what AI implementation actually looks like on the ground. Using real client case studies (a realtor who went from voicemail to 24/7 AI-qualified leads, a civil engineering firm that doubled its proposal win rate from 30% to 60%, and a hiring platform that gives every candidate an interview instead of ghosting 1,597 of them), she demonstrates where AI creates measurable, quantifiable business impact.
takeaways: A realtor switched from print mailers to a QR code + AI voice agent: calls answered 24/7, leads qualified instantly, calendar booked automatically
A civil engineering firm cut proposal backlog and raised win rate from 30% to 60% by letting AI draft proposals for human approval
AI hiring platforms give every candidate a real interview — ending the recruiter bias of only reading the top three resumes
The gap between doers and complainers is widening. The doers are already deploying this
Start with one process that is slow, expensive, or inconsistent — that is your first AI win
What would happen to your business if you suddenly had 150 waiting clients? Build the capacity before you need it
who: Operators and founders ready to implement AI in a specific workflow, not just talk about it
brief: AI Operations Expert and Co-Founder of Key Singularity Kristi Galus on the real-world implementations that move the needle.

title: Creative Direction and Marketing That Moves
synopsis: Whitney Smith reveals how creative direction and expert marketing work together to build brands people remember.
who: Business owners who want a stronger brand presence
brief: Creative Director & Marketing Expert Whitney Smith on making your brand impossible to ignore.

title: The Choreography of High Performance
synopsis: Taylor James Randall draws on cinematography and visual storytelling to teach entrepreneurs how to present their brand with impact.
who: Entrepreneurs looking to sharpen their visual and stage presence
brief: Cinematographer Taylor James Randall on the art of showing up powerfully on camera and on stage.

title: Venture Capital and the TEDx Mindset
synopsis: Maxime Andre Moulema Douala brings a venture capital lens and TEDx-stage experience to help entrepreneurs think bigger and pitch better.
who: Entrepreneurs seeking capital or looking to expand their reach
brief: Venture Capitalist and Founder of TEDx Harvard Square Maxime Andre on thinking and pitching at the highest level.

title: Health as a Business Advantage
synopsis: Dr. Rajaipour connects entrepreneur health and peak physical performance directly to business outcomes.
brief: Founder of VitaRegen Medical Dr. Negin Rajaipour on why your health is your most valuable business asset.

title: HeroWorks: Vending as a Scalable Business
synopsis: Jason Funaro presents HeroWorks' model for turning vending machine businesses into scalable, semi-passive income streams.
brief: Co-Founder of HeroWorks Jason Funaro on scaling vending into a real business.

title: HeroWorks: Operations and Growth
synopsis: Brenda Roh covers the operational side of HeroWorks — logistics, placement, and growing a vending portfolio.
who: Entrepreneurs interested in vending business operations
brief: Co-Founder of HeroWorks Brenda Roh on the day-to-day of running and growing a vending business.
